title: Making predictions with probability
description: Practice predicting the number of times a certain event will happen.
topic_path: Get ready for probability and combinatorics

assessment_items.question_content:
Cam is going to roll a fair $6$-sided die $2400$ times.

**What is the best prediction for the number of times that Cam will roll the number $4$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of an individual roll landing on the number $4$.

${\text{Best prediction}} = {\text{ Number of trials}} \times {\text{P(roll a 4)}}$

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{2400}$

$\text{P(roll a 4)} = \pink{\dfrac{1}{6}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{2400}\times\pink{\dfrac{1}{6}}=400$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times Cam will roll the number $4$ because rolling a die is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Cam will roll the number $4$ *close to* $400$ times but probably not exactly $400$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $400$ times
*Close to* $400$ times but probably not exactly $400$ times
*Exactly* $600$ times
*Close to* $600$ times but probably not exactly $600$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Kavitha is going to spin the following spinner $200$ times. The spinner is divided into equal sections. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the best prediction for the number of times the spinner will land on the elephant?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of spins by the probability of an individual spin landing on the elephant.

$\text{Best prediction} =$ $ {\text{ Number of spins}} \times$ $\text{P(land on the elephant)}$

$\text{Number of spins} = \blue{200}$

$\text{P(land on the elephant)}= \pink{\dfrac{1}{5}}$
$\text{Best prediction} =\blue{200} \times\pink{\dfrac{1}{5}}=40$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times the spinner will land on the elephant because spinning a spinner is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that the spinner will land on the elephant *close to* $40$ times but probably not exactly $40$ times.

options.alt: A circle has five equal sections. There is a monkey in two sections, a mouse in two sections, and an elephant in one section. The arrow of the spinner points at the elephant section.

choices.content: *Exactly* $80$ times
*Close to* $80$ times but probably not exactly $80$ times
*Exactly* $40$ times
*Close to* $40$ times but probably not exactly $40$ times

**What is the best prediction for the number of times that the coin will land heads up?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of an individual flip landing heads up.

$\text{Best prediction} = {\text{ Number of trials}} \times {\text{P(land heads up)}}$

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{1200}$

$\text{P(land heads up)} = \pink{\dfrac{1}{2}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{1200}\times\pink{\dfrac{1}{2}}=600$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times the coin will land heads up because flipping a coin is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that the coin will land heads up *close to* $600$ times but probably not exactly $600$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $50$ times
*Close to* $50$ times but probably not exactly $50$ times
*Exactly* $600$ times
*Close to* $600$ times but probably not exactly $600$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Ellen has a bag with $3$ red marbles and $2$ blue marbles in it.    She is going to randomly draw a marble from the bag $300$ times, putting the marble back in the bag after each draw.

![3 red marbles and 2 blue marbles](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3d698c75caf85dcd1e92ce4a18e07a59.png)

**What is the best prediction for the number of times that Ellen will draw a blue marble?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of Ellen getting a blue marble on an individual draw.

$\text{Best prediction} = {\text{ Number of trials}} \times {\text{P(blue marble)}}$

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{300}$

$\text{P(blue marble)} = \pink{\dfrac{2}{5}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{300}\times \pink{\dfrac25}=120$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times Ellen will draw a blue marble because drawing a marble is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Ellen will draw a blue marble *close to* $120$ times but probably not exactly $120$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $120$ times
*Close to* $120$ times but probably not exactly $120$ times
*Exactly* $150$ times
*Close to* $150$ times but probably not exactly $150$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Amit is going to guess on all $20$ questions of his multiple-choice test.  Each question on the test has $4$ answer options, and only one is correct.

**What is the best prediction for the number of questions that Amit will answer correctly?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of questions by the probability of Amit getting an individual question correct.

$\text{Best prediction} = {\text{ Number of questions}}\times {\text{P(correct answer)}} $

$\text{Number of questions} = \blue{20}$

$\text{P(correct answer)} = \pink{\dfrac{1}{4}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{20}\times\pink{\dfrac{1}{4}}=5$
We should not predict *exactly* how many questions Amit will answer correctly because guessing on a test is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Amit will answer *close to* $5$ questions but probably not exactly $5$ questions correctly.

choices.content: *Exactly* $5$ questions
*Close to* $5$ questions but probably not exactly $5$ questions
*Exactly* $80$ questions
*Close to* $80$ questions but probably not exactly $80$ questions

assessment_items.question_content:
Leo is going to use a random number generator $400$ times. Each time he uses it, he will get a $1, 2, 3,4,$ or $5$.

**What is the best prediction for the number of times that Leo will get an odd number?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of Leo getting an odd number on an individual number generation.

$\text{Best prediction} = \text{ Number of trials} \times  {\text{P(odd number)}} $

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{400}$

$\text{P(odd number)} = \pink{\dfrac{3}{5}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{400}\times\pink{\dfrac{3}{5}}=240$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times Leo will get an odd number because using a random number generator is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Leo will get an odd number *close to* $240$ times but probably not exactly $240$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $133$ times
*Close to* $133$ times but probably not exactly $133$ times
*Exactly* $240$ times
*Close to* $240$ times but probably not exactly $240$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Chen has an MP3 player called the Jumble. The Jumble randomly selects a song for the user to listen to. Chen's Jumble has $4$ classical songs, $3$ rock songs, and $2$ rap songs on it. Chen is going to listen to $360$ songs.

**What is the best prediction for the number of times Chen will listen to a classical song?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of songs by the probability of an individual song being a classical song.


$\text{Best prediction} = \text{ Number of songs} \times \text{P(classical song)}$

$\text{Number of songs} = \blue{360}$

>$\text{P(classical song)} = \pink{\dfrac{4}{9}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{360}\times\pink{\dfrac{4}{9}}=160$

We should not predict *exactly* how many times Chen will listen to a classical song because the Jumble selects a song randomly. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Chen will listen to a classical song *close to* $160$ times but probably not exactly $160$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $90$ times
*Close to* $90$ times but probably not exactly $90$ times
*Exactly* $160$ times
*Close to* $160$ times but probably not exactly $160$ times

assessment_items.question_content:

**What is the best prediction for the number of times that Jeremy  will roll a number greater than  $4$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

${\text{Best prediction}} = {\text{ Number of trials}} \times {\text{P(greater than  4)}}$

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{180}$

$\text{P(greater than 4)} = \dfrac{2}{6} = \pink{\dfrac{1}{3}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{180}\times\pink{\dfrac{1}{3}}=60$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times Jeremy  will roll a number greater than  $4$ because rolling a die is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Jeremy  will roll a number greater than  $4$ *close to* $60$ times but probably not exactly $60$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $30$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Jose is going to use a random number generator $500$ times. Each time he uses it, he will get a $1, 2, 3,$ or $4$.

**Complete the following statement with the *best* prediction.    
Jose will get something other than a $2$...**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of Jose getting something other than a $2$ on an individual number generation.


$\text{Best prediction} =   {\text{ Number of trials}}\times \text{P(not get a 2)} $
$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{500}$

$\text{P(not get a 2)} = \pink{\dfrac{3}{4}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{500}\times\pink{\dfrac{3}{4}}=375$
We should not predict *exactly* how many times Jose will get something other than a $2$ because using a random number generator is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Jose will get something other than a $2$ *close to* $375$ times but probably not exactly $375$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $250$ times
*Close to* $250$ times but probably not exactly $250$ times
*Exactly* $375$ times
*Close to* $375$ times but probably not exactly $375$ times

 Vitor will correctly answer...**

$\text{Number of questions} = \blue{40}$

$\text{P(correct answer)} = \pink{\dfrac{1}{5}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{40}\times\pink{\dfrac{1}{5}}=8$
We should not predict *exactly* how many questions Vitor will answer correctly because guessing on a test is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ming has a standard $52$-card deck.  A standard $52$-card deck has $4$ suits $(♠$, $♣ ,\red♦$, and $\red♥$$)$ and an equal number of cards of each suit.  She is going to randomly draw a card from the deck $300$ times, putting the card back in the deck after each draw.

**Complete the following statement with the *best* prediction.   
Ming will draw something other than a $\Large\red♥$…**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of trials by the probability of Ming not drawing a $\red♥$ on an individual draw.


$\text{Best prediction} =   {\text{ Number of trials}}\times\text{P(draw card other than a }\red{\text{♥}})$

$\text{Number of trials} = \blue{300}$


$\text{P(draw card other than a }\red{\text{♥}}\text{)}= \pink{\dfrac{3}{4}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{300}\times\pink{\dfrac34}=225$

We should not predict *exactly* how many times Ming will draw something other than a $\red♥$ because drawing a card is random. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The *best* prediction is that she will draw something other than a $\red♥$ *close to* $225$ times but probably not exactly $225$ times.

choices.content: *Exactly* $150$ times
*Close to* $150$ times but probably not exactly $150$ times
*Exactly* $225$ times
*Close to* $225$ times but probably not exactly $225$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
Mekala has an MP3 player called the Jumble. The Jumble randomly selects a song for the user to listen to. Mekala's Jumble has $4$ techno songs, $2$ country songs, and $3$ jazz songs on it. Mekala plans to listen to $270$ songs.

**Complete the following statement with the *best* prediction.    
Mekala will listen to...**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of songs by the probability of an individual song being a country song.


$\text{Best prediction} =  {\text{ Number of songs}}\times\text{P(country song)} $

$\text{Number of songs} = \blue{270}$

$\text{P(country song)} = \pink{\dfrac{2}{9}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{270}\times\pink{\dfrac{2}{9}}=60$
We should not predict *exactly* how many country songs she will listen to because  the Jumble selects a song randomly. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that Mekala will listen to *close to* $60$ country songs but probably not exactly $60$ country songs.

choices.content: *Exactly* $60$ country songs
*Close to* $60$ country songs but probably not exactly $60$ country songs
*Exactly* $210$ country songs
*Close to* $210$ country songs but probably not exactly $210$ country songs

assessment_items.question_content:
Electronics Unlimited sells TVs. There are $110$ TVs on display in the showroom, and each TV is turned on to a random channel from a set of $11$ channels: $7$ sports channels, $3$ news channels, and $1$ movie channel.

**Which statement best predicts how many TVs will *not* be showing a sports channel?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of TVs by the probability of an individual TV not showing a sports channel.


$\text{Best prediction} = {\text{ Number of TVs}}\times\text{P(not sports)}$

$\text{Number of TVs} = \blue{110}$

$\text{P(not sports)} = \pink{\dfrac{4}{11}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{110}\times\pink{\dfrac{4}{11}}=40$

We should not predict *exactly* how many TVs will not be showing a sports channel because each TV is turned to a random channel. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The *best* prediction is that there will be *close to* $40$ TVs but probably not exactly $40$ TVs not showing a sports channel.

choices.content: There will be *exactly* $40$ TVs not showing a sports channel.
There will be *close to* $40$ TVs but probably not exactly $40$ TVs not showing a sports channel.
There will be *exactly* $70$ TVs not showing a sports channel.
There will be *close to* $70$ TVs but probably not exactly $70$ TVs not showing a sports channel.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Hendricksons are in charge of bringing treats for $50$ football players.  They brought $3$ coolers of popsicles and $2$ coolers of ice cream sandwiches.  Each player is going to randomly select a cooler from which to get their treat. 

**Which statement best predicts how many football players will get a popsicle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The best prediction comes from multiplying the total number of football players by the probability of an individual football player getting a popsicle. 


$\text{Best prediction} =  \text{Number of football players} \times\text{P(popsicle)}$

$\text{Number of football players} = \blue{50}$

$\text{P(popsicle)} = \pink{\dfrac{3}{5}}$
$\text{Best prediction} = \blue{50}\times\pink{\dfrac{3}{5}}=30$
We should not predict *exactly* how many players will get a popsicle because each player randomly selects a cooler. It is impossible to make exact predictions for outcomes of random events.
The best prediction is that there will be *close to* $30$ football players but probably not exactly $30$ football players who get a popsicle.

choices.content: There will be *exactly* $20$ football players who get a popsicle.
There will be *close to* $20$ football players but probably not exactly $20$ football players who get a popsicle.
There will be *exactly* $30$ football players who get a popsicle.
There will be *close to* $30$ football players but probably not exactly $30$ football players who get a popsicle.
